causes: suction line blocked or valve closed
loss of suction (empty tank, low level)
consequences: if pump continues running with no flow: overheating of pump casing/seal damage
reduced discharge pressure (natural coupling in centrifugal curve)

causes: downstream demand increase / control valve wide open
consequences: reduced discharge pressure (per pump curve, not overpressure risk)

causes: pump stopped with downstream pressure higher than pump shutoff head
consequences: reverse rotation of pump (potential mechanical damage)

causes: insufficient NPSH available (low suction pressure, high suction lift, high fluid temperature)
vapor pressure of fluid approached at suction
downstream blockage (note: centrifugal pumps are inherently limited by shutoff head — overpressure risk is bounded, not a primary hazard like PD pumps)
consequences: operation at shutoff head — overheating if prolonged, not typically a rupture hazard

causes: suction line blocked or valve closed
internal slip/wear (worn gears, rotors, or diaphragm)
consequences: reduced metering accuracy (if used for dosing)

causes: discharge valve closed or line blocked while pump continues running (deadhead condition)
consequences: SEVERE: piping/pump casing rupture — PD pumps generate unbounded pressure against a closed discharge, unlike centrifugal pumps

causes: pump stopped, downstream pressure higher than upstream
consequences: reverse mechanical loading on pump internals
possible cavitation-like damage (less common than centrifugal but possible)
